name: Web Theme — Tokens, Schemes & High Contrast
summary: The webtheme standard proven in a real browser: the DTCG token document compiles to native CSS at runtime (compileToCss → :root custom properties), and the derived scheme + accent ramp emit under color-scheme with a @media (prefers-contrast: more) high-contrast pair (compileSchemeCss). Sample components are styled only by the emitted variables; live controls switch scheme and high-contrast so the emitted CSS is visibly the thing doing the work — the in-browser conformance proof the #1097 completion sub-epic wanted.
description:
Concept

An in-browser conformance proof for Web Theme: the DTCG token document is compiled to native CSS at runtime — a :root custom-property block via compileToCss, plus a derived scheme + accent ramp under color-scheme and a @media (prefers-contrast: more) high-contrast pair via compileSchemeCss.

The point

Every surface on the page is styled only by the emitted custom properties. The live scheme and high-contrast toggles switch the same emitted CSS, so you can see the contrast-gated scheme pair and the white/black HC extremes do the work — not a hand-authored stylesheet.

name: Mockup-to-Standard Playground
summary: The #086 design-to-code front door on the #094 upgrader pipeline: each card lifts a UI mockup (static image, Figma frame, interactive prototype) through the SAME engine and SAME verify gate — mockup → neutral ComponentIR → generated <component> → verify badge → live element. Mockup analysis is a swappable, no-leakage CustomVisionProvider behind the registry; a deterministic keyless reference provider stands in until the Plateau vision service (#475) ships. No parallel generator — the generated <component> is the exact declarative form the adapters consume.
description:
Concept

A live surface for backlog #086 — a design-to-code tool that ingests a UI mockup and emits Web Everything standard-compliant code. It is one more input adapter on the shared #094 pipeline: a mockup lifts to the same neutral ComponentIR and rides the same verify-gated <component> generation a legacy-code input does.

Two invariants made visible

AI-tool independence: mockup analysis is a swappable CustomVisionProvider behind the registry — any model/tool meeting the contract drops in. Vision is a Plateau service consumed no-leakage (#475): only the neutral structure and the verified output reach the standard. A deterministic keyless reference provider stands in until that service ships.

Same core

The generated <component> mounts as a real custom element — proof the tool is a front door onto the same transform the adapters document, not a competing codegen island.

name: Auto Insurance (exercise app B)
summary: A full, complex personal-auto insurance app — the second flagship exercise app (#314/#318), in a modern-SaaS register. Slice S0 (foundation) is live: a 4,000-policy seeded book is rated + underwritten client-side (a per-policy premium breakdown and an underwriting decision-trace), rendered through the SAME shipped blocks the loan app drove — data-table, pagination, master-detail, decision-trace, status-indicator, lifecycle, audit, router — making insurance their second consumer (a cross-app conformance check). The quote wizard, coverage tree, and claims follow.
description:
Concept

Candidate B of the flagship exercise-apps program — a personal-auto policy lifecycle (quote → bind → issue → endorse → renew → cancel) plus FNOL/claims, chosen for its deeply conditional questionnaires, coverage hierarchy, rating/underwriting rules, and roles. Approximated, not a compliant rating plan. Requirements: requirements report. Tracked by epic #318.

Live now — S0 foundation

The domain core: a rating engine (multiplicative factors → 6-month premium) and an underwriting rules engine that emits a per-policy decision-trace, a deterministic 4k-policy book seed, and two entity lifecycles (policy + claim). The harness rates the whole book client-side and renders the finding distribution plus a clickable per-policy detail.

The point: a second consumer

Insurance reuses the standards the loan app drove (lifecycle, status-indicator, audit, decision-trace, master-detail, data-table, pagination, selection, router) in a different domain and a different visual register — proving the platform reskins and the standards compose across apps. It will drive the next gap set: tree-select, configurator, file handling, view-transitions, permissions, persistence.

name: Loan Origination (exercise app A)
summary: A full, complex mortgage loan-origination app built to exercise the standard end-to-end (flagship exercise-app program, backlog #314/#317). Slice S0 (foundation) is live: 5,000 seeded applications run through a rules-as-data underwriting engine in ~19ms, with an AUS-style finding spread and a per-loan proof-of-compliance trace. Borrower 1003 wizard, document checklist, product/rate configurator, and the underwriter workbench follow.
description:
Concept

Candidate A of the flagship exercise-apps program — a residential mortgage origination flow (application → clear-to-close) chosen because it stresses the whole standard: complex multi-step validation, codifiable business rules, identity/permissions, persistence, a product configurator, file handling, and a pipeline at scale. Structure is anchored on real industry shape (URLA/1003, DTI/LTV, AUS findings, TRID) but approximated, not a compliant LOS. Requirements: requirements report.

Live now — S0 foundation

The framework-agnostic domain core: a rules-as-data engine (JSON predicate DSL, versioned, AUS-style aggregation) that emits a per-rule proof-of-compliance trace, a product catalog + rate sheet with LLPA pricing, derived underwriting facts (DTI/LTV/reserves), a deterministic 5k-application seed, and a mock provider seam (credit/AUS/verification/e-sign/doc-classify). The harness generates and evaluates all 5,000 applications client-side and renders the finding distribution plus a clickable trace per loan.

Built against the standard

Hosted under demos/loan-origination/ on the declarative-SPA shell so the app dogfoods the standard's own routing, loader, components, and intents as later slices land (S1 permissions → S2 lifecycle → S3 the 1003 wizard).

name: Loader → Background Task Handoff Playground
summary: The producer half of the Background Task Surface handoff: a real Resource Loader running backgroundLoad() escalates a slow load into a live <background-tasks> rail when it crosses the debounce threshold. Each card drives the real pair (not a scripted mock) and a badge proves an invariant — slow loads escalate and resolve, fast loads never register, failures are sticky and retry re-runs the real load. Scenarios shared with the unit suite. Backlog #152.
description:
Concept

The producer half of the Background Task Surface handoff (#135 built the receiving surface; this wires the source). A real Resource Loader running backgroundLoad() escalates a slow load into a live <background-tasks> rail, where it is tracked off-view with progress, completion announcement, and per-task retry.

Escalation:async trigger

A load escalates exactly when it crosses the Loader's debounce threshold into loading — the moment the Loader already decided the work was slow enough to surface a pending UI, reused rather than inventing a new threshold. Fast loads that resolve first never enter loading, so they never reach the rail. Backgrounding is opt-in: call backgroundLoad() instead of load().

Verified contract

An adapter projects the Loader's own events (resource-state-change / resource-load-end / resource-load-error) onto the surface's getSnapshot / subscribe / retry handle, registered via a bubbling background-task-register event. The cards prove: a slow load escalates to an active entry then resolves to success, a fast load never registers, and a failed load is sticky with a retry affordance that re-runs the real loader.load().

No drift

The scenarios and the controllable-promise lever come from the same shared fixture module the unit suite imports, so the badges here and CI exercise the same behavior. This is the route-only baseline; the reload-durable tier is the separate #134 Background Fetch adapter.

name: Module-as-a-Service Playground
summary: Serve one authored <component> in any form — declarative source, Web Component class, template HTML, or JSX — chosen by a single request param. Each card has a form toggle and a live element registered from the served WC form. Backlog #081.
description:
Concept

A live surface for backlog #081 — a provider that serves one authored component in any form via a single request param. Pick a form per card and the same <component> definition is returned as declarative source, a Web Component class, template HTML, or JSX; the live element is registered from the served WC form.

No drift

The resolver owns no transform — it dispatches to the same shared transforms the rendering adapters use, so the served form provably matches the documented one. Unsupported params (transpileTarget, strategy) are flagged, never silently dropped.

Scope

v1 walking skeleton: a single synchronous resolve. Reactivity and ESM-over-HTTP delivery are phase 2 — see the Consumer demo for native import.

name: Module-as-a-Service Consumer
summary: A consumer with no build step: a native import('/_maas/user-card.js?form=wc-class') returns a self-contained ES module that registers the element on evaluation — the server applied the adapter transform. Backlog #081 phase 2a.
description:
Concept

The native-only seam of backlog #081, phase 2a. This page is a consumer with no build step: it does a native import('/_maas/user-card.js?form=wc-class') and the returned ES module registers the element on evaluation. The server applied the adapter transform — nothing is bundled or transpiled on the consumer.

Scope

v1 serves the wc-class form (self-contained ESM). The jsx form needs the delegated transpile step (phase 2b) before it is import-able. Import-map / bare-specifier resolution starts to matter only once a served module imports another.

name: Code Upgrader Playground
summary: The inverse of Module-as-a-Service: take EXISTING legacy code and lift it onto the standard. Each card runs the shared engine — legacy source → neutral structure (IR) → generated <component> → verify gate → live element. The AI analyzer is a swappable provider (MVP ships a deterministic reference one, no key); output is only offered when it re-parses, round-trips without drift, and references real intents. Backlog #094.
description:
Concept

A live surface for backlog #094 — an AI upgrader that takes existing/legacy code and lifts it onto the standard. It is the inverse of Module-as-a-Service: MaaS serves one authored component in many forms; the upgrader produces the authored <component> from a legacy component in the first place. Each card shows the whole pipeline: legacy source → neutral structure (IR) → generated <component> → verify gate → live element.

AI is a swappable provider

The analyzer (code → IR) is registry-backed — the same inject-a-provider shape as the MaaS compiler seam. This MVP injects a deterministic reference provider (no model key) that handles one source path (vanilla web components); a bring-your-own-key model provider drops in for messier input with no other change.

The verify gate is the moat

Output is only offered when it re-parses, round-trips to the analyzed structure without drift, and references only real intents. Generic AI emits plausible code; this emits checked code. Un-verified output is shown for inspection but never presented as trusted — and the generated <component> feeds the same MaaS serve() core, so it is a front door onto the standard, not a parallel generator.

name: Conformance Auto-Fix Playground
summary: The interactive surface over the conformance auto-fix loop (#095/#196/#293): drive a failing conformance suite back to green, card by card. Each card runs the real autofix orchestrator — failure (check:standards) → proposed patch → diff → verify gate → accept/revert. The verify gate is the moat: a patch is kept only if its target failure cleared and it introduced no new error, else reverted automatically. AI is a swappable provider (a scripted no-key model fixer runs through the same registry a BYO-key client would), with a model-fix cost bound. Backlog #298.
description:
Concept

A live surface for backlog #298 — the interactive front end on the conformance auto-fix loop. It is the sibling of the Code Upgrader, one domain over: the upgrader lifts legacy code onto the standard; this drives a failing conformance suite back to green. Each card runs the whole loop: failure → proposed patch → diff → verify gate → accept / revert, driving the same autofix orchestrator the CLI and unit suite use over an in-memory world.

The verify gate is the moat

A proposed patch is applied, the suite re-run, and the patch kept only if its target failure cleared and it introduced no new error — else reverted automatically. A plausible-but-wrong fix is never shipped.

AI is a swappable provider

A fixer (failure → patch) drops into the same registry whether it is the deterministic reference fixer or a bring-your-own-key model fixer. The model cards run a scripted no-key client through the exact seam a real Anthropic client would use, gated by the same verify pass and the same model-fix cost bound.

name: Component Adapter Playground
summary: Live test surface for the declarative <component> element and its build-time AST transform: each definition shows its authored source, the generated class the adapter emits, and a live-registered element — with a conformance badge. Includes an editable sandbox to try your own definitions.
description:
Concept

This playground exercises the <component> standard and its intended implementation, the Declarative Component adapter. For each definition it shows three panes: the authored declarative source, the generated class the deterministic build-time transform would emit, and a live element registered from that definition — then checks that the rendered shadow/light tree equals the authored template (pass/fail badge).

Test your own

The editable sandbox parses a <component> definition plus its usage, emits the class, registers the element under a unique tag (so repeated runs with edits work), and renders it live.

Scope

Runs the runtime twin of the build-time AST transform so it can be exercised in a browser — tier 1: static template, slots, and shadow modes (open/closed/none). Reactive bindings and scoped registration are tier-2 and compose with other standards later.

name: JSX Adapter Playground
summary: Mirror-dialect sandbox: authored JSX rendered live to DOM through the realigned JSX renderer, shown beside its produced HTML. The iteration surface for the HTML↔JSX feature mapping before the source-toggle lands on block pages.
description:
Concept

Each card authors a tree in the JSX mirror dialect — a faithful, reversible spelling of the canonical HTML adapter (class not className, for not htmlFor, on:* string behaviors, bind-* bindings, <template is="…"> directives) — renders it live through the realigned JSX renderer, and shows the produced HTML beside it.

Why a sandbox

This is the development surface for the HTML↔JSX feature mapping: improve the example pairs and the renderer here, in isolation, before bringing the source-toggle onto real block pages. It exercises the realigned JSXRenderer end-to-end in a browser (customized built-ins via is, namespaced behavior attributes, class/for first-class).

Scope

The sandbox focuses on structural JSX→DOM/HTML equivalence. Behavior activation (events firing beyond the demo's own listener, bind-* updating, is="for-each" iterating) belongs to the rendering-strategy axis, which is parked.

name: JSX Directive Sugar Playground
summary: The <For each>/<Show when>/<Resource from> sugar layer (#070): each card renders the prettier components live, proving they build the same DOM as the canonical <template is="…"> directive, and shows desugar() lowering them back. Fixture-driven, backed by the conformance suite.
description:
Concept

The pretty <For each> / <Show when> / <Resource from> components are optional sugar for the canonical <template is="…"> directives — an alternative spelling of the same directive, mapped through one registry (see the JSX Adapter). Each card authors the sugar, renders it live through the runtime directive components, checks the produced DOM equals the canonical directive's DOM, and shows desugar() lowering it to <template is> plus the sugarize() round-trip.

One registry, three surfaces

The desugar transform, the sugarize lift, and the runtime components all read the same directive registry, so a new directive is one entry and the spelling can never drift from the canonical form.

Scope

A spelling layer (Axis-1): <For> builds an inert <template is="for-each"> exactly as the canonical spelling does. Actually expanding the loop is the parked rendering-strategy axis (Axis-2).

name: Declarative Single-Page Application
summary: A fully interactive SPA using only declarative HTML with zero JavaScript bootstrap or custom elements. Demonstrates maximum interactivity through pure markup and Web Everything standards.
description:
Concept

This demo explores the limits of declarative, zero-JavaScript interactivity by building a complete single-page application that is served as static HTML in the initial HTTP request. No framework bootstrap, no custom elements registration, no JavaScript imports—just HTML enhanced with Web Everything attributes.

Key Constraints

  • No JavaScript Bootstrap: The HTML document loads and is interactive immediately without waiting for JS bundles.
  • No Custom Elements: Uses only native HTML elements enhanced with behavioral attributes.
  • Server-Rendered: The complete application structure is returned in the initial HTTP response.
  • Progressive Enhancement: Core functionality works before any JavaScript loads; behaviors enhance the experience.

Interactive Features

  • State Management: Declarative context definitions using <script type="context">
  • Data Binding: Two-way binding with bind-text, bind-value attributes
  • Event Handling: Actions triggered via on:click, on:input without inline JavaScript
  • Conditional Rendering: Elements shown/hidden based on context state
  • List Rendering: Dynamic lists generated from context data
  • Form Validation: Declarative validation rules without custom code

Technical Approach

The demo leverages Web Contexts for state management, Web Directives for declarative behaviors, and Web Injectors for dependency resolution—all configured through HTML attributes and inline context definitions. Behaviors are attached via attribute-based protocols that activate on page load, transforming static HTML into an interactive application.

name: Router Demo
summary: Declarative client-side routing with guards, loaders, error boundaries, and named outlets — all driven by HTML templates inside a single <route-view> element.
description:
Concept

This demo showcases a fully declarative client-side router built on the Navigation API and URLPattern. Routes are defined as <template route="/path"> children of a <route-view> element, with matched content stamped in-place.

Key Features

  • Declarative Routes: Define routes as <template> elements with a route attribute — no JavaScript router configuration needed.
  • Route Guards: Protect routes with route:guard attributes. Guards can allow, block, or redirect navigation.
  • Route Loaders: Fetch async data before rendering via route:loader. Loader results populate the stamped content.
  • Loader Intent: Declare loading strategy (soft, hard, replacement, optimistic) and timing (debounced, immediate) via customContexts:loaderIntent. Switch strategies live to see different loading behaviors. Per-route overrides via route:loader-strategy attribute.
  • Error Boundaries: Define route:error templates to handle loader failures gracefully.
  • Named Outlets: Render auxiliary content in <route-outlet> elements for sidebars, toolbars, etc.
  • Navigation Links: route:link behavior on <a> elements intercepts clicks for SPA navigation with active class management.
  • Prefetching: route:prefetch="hover" warms the loader cache on hover for instant navigation.

Technical Approach

The router uses the Navigation API (with History API fallback) for URL interception, URLPattern for route matching, and the Web Everything injector system for guard/loader resolution. Templates are stamped and unstamped as the user navigates, with route context available to event handlers via custom events. Loading states are driven by the Loader Intent protocol — a single declaration that controls how pending states affect the UI.

name: View + Tabs Demo
summary: ViewEngine for show/hide with multiple hidden modes, exclusive groups, and toggle events. TabGroupBehavior for accessible tabbed interfaces with ARIA roles and keyboard navigation.
description:
Concept

This demo showcases the View and Tabs blocks — the foundation for all show/hide patterns in Web Everything.

Key Features

  • ViewEngine: Shared primitive for show/hide/toggle with 4 hidden modes (display, content-visibility, until-found, inert).
  • Toggle Events: Popover API-compatible beforetoggle (cancelable) and toggle events.
  • Exclusive Groups: Elements with the same name attribute — only one visible at a time, mirroring <details name>.
  • Tabs: Accessible tabbed interface with ARIA roles (tablist/tab/tabpanel), keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Home/End), and automatic/manual activation modes.
  • Configuration: Live switching between activation modes (automatic/manual) and orientations (horizontal/vertical).

Technical Approach

Tabs is a thin ARIA + keyboard layer over ViewEngine. Panels are always in the DOM (behavior pattern), with visibility managed by the engine. State preservation is automatic since elements are never destroyed.

name: Text Interpolation Demo
summary: Text node expression pipeline with {{mustache}} and [[polymer]] syntax, named contexts, pipes, nested paths, and template instantiation — all rendered without FOUC.
description:
Concept

This demo showcases the complete text node expression pipeline: text node parsers detect {{ }} and [[ ]] syntax, expression parsers evaluate content, and InterpolationTextNode renders resolved values into the DOM.

Key Features

  • Basic Interpolation: {{name}} renders state values from injector-provided contexts.
  • Nested Paths: {{user.profile.name}} with dot-notation resolution.
  • Named Contexts: {{@theme.primary}} resolves values from named injector contexts.
  • Pipe Expressions: {{name | uppercase}} applies filter functions via the pipe parser.
  • Multiple Expressions: Several expressions in the same text node.
  • Polymer Syntax: [[count]] using double-square-bracket parser.
  • Mixed Syntax: Both {{ }} and [[ ]] on the same page.
  • Template Instantiation: Expressions inside <template> elements, cloned at runtime to avoid FOUC.

Technical Approach

The pipeline flows: Text → CustomTextNodeParser → UndeterminedTextNode → CustomTextNodeRegistry upgrade → InterpolationTextNode → CustomExpressionParserRegistry → Resolved Value. Parsers are registered via bootstrap ('mustache' for {{ }}, 'polymer' for [[ ]]).

name: Navigation Demo
description: Accessible sidebar navigation with collapsible sections, keyboard navigation, and router integration.

name: Injector Runtime API
summary: InjectableModule provides app-level services with lazy loading via register(), InjectorRoot bridges module→DOM, await consume() resolves like import through DI, dispose clears providers.
description:
Concept

This demo validates the Injector Runtime API by exercising the full lifecycle: module-level provider registration (eager and lazy), cross-scope bridging to the DOM, async consumption via await consume(), and clean disposal.

Key Features

  • InjectableModule: Factory pattern for module-level dependency injection via new InjectableModule(name, import.meta, setup).
  • Cross-Scope Bridging: InjectorRoot.attach(document, module.injector) wires ModuleInjector as parent of HTMLInjector.
  • Lazy Loading: register(key, loader) declares providers that are loaded on first consume().
  • Async Consumption: await consume(key, querier) works like import() but resolved through DI — returns the value directly.
  • Provider Lifecycle: dispose() clears all providers, registrations, and in-flight loads.

name: For-Each Demo
summary: Structural directive that stamps a <template> for each item in a collection, using comment markers and injector-based context for {{@item.name}} interpolation.
description:
Concept

This demo showcases the for-each directive — a structural directive that iterates over collections and stamps template content for each item. Each stamped item receives its own injector context, so standard text interpolation with {{@alias.path}} works automatically.

Key Features

  • Comment Markers: Uses comment nodes (no wrapper element) to preserve CSS Grid/Flex parent-child relationships.
  • "items as item" Syntax: PHP/Blade-style alias for the loop context.
  • Injector Context: Each stamped item gets customContexts:{alias} on its root element's injector.
  • Text Interpolation: Standard {{@member.name}} resolves through the injector chain.
  • Multiple Elements Per Item: Templates can stamp <dt>/<dd> pairs for definition lists.

name: Pagination Playground
summary: The runnable home for the Pagination block — the blocks-layer realization of the Collection Operations `page` stage. Each card pairs a page-state with a contract (pageMode × advance, urlSync, rangeLabel), renders the controls, and runs the same a11y/SEO audit the CI conformance suite asserts. Backlog #036.
description:
Concept

A live surface for the Pagination block, which realizes the page stage of the Collection Operations intent. For each shared fixture it shows the contract options, renders the controls through the reference renderer, shows the produced HTML, and runs the contract audit — a green badge means the rendered controls satisfy the verified accessibility/SEO contract.

Verified contract

The audit checks the WAI-ARIA APG pattern — a <nav aria-label="pagination"> landmark with aria-current="page" — plus the SEO-safe self-canonical ?page=n default, the result-range label's known-total requirement, and infinite scroll rendered as a scroll sentinel (append + auto), never a peer mode.

No drift

The renderer, the audit, and the fixtures are the one shared source the CI conformance suite runs, so the badges here and CI exercise the exact same code. The renderer is a deterministic reference; concrete strategies (a real URL-state provider, windowing) live in Frontier UI.

name: Data Table Playground
summary: The runnable home for the Data Table block — the blocks-layer realization of the Collection Operations `filter` / `sort` / `group` stages. Each card pairs a row set with a contract (sort keys + Intl.Collator options, filterMatch, groupSummary), renders the table, and runs the same APG / Intl.Collator / SQL-aggregate audit the CI conformance suite asserts. Plus a live interactive card: click a header to cycle its sort, toggle the filter, and hear the change announced through a polite `aria-live` region. Backlog #112, #115.
description:
Concept

A live surface for the Data Table block, which realizes the filter, sort, and group stages of the Collection Operations intent (the page stage is the companion Pagination block — compose, never merge). For each shared fixture it shows the contract options, renders the table through the reference renderer, shows the produced HTML, and runs the contract audit — a green badge means the rendered table satisfies the verified accessibility / comparison contract.

Verified contract

The audit checks the WAI-ARIA APG Sortable Table pattern — a native <table> with <th scope="col"> headers, the active column projecting aria-sort, sortable-but-unsorted headers at none — plus Intl.Collator ordering (numeric / sensitivity), SQL NULLS FIRST/LAST empty placement, filterMatch AND/OR narrowing, and per-group SQL-named groupSummary aggregates.

No drift

The renderer, the audit, and the fixtures are the one shared source the CI conformance suite runs, so the badges here and CI exercise the exact same code. The renderer is a deterministic reference; concrete strategies (an alternate comparator, server-delegated ordering, virtualization) live in Frontier UI.

name: Reorderable List Playground
summary: The runnable home for the Reorderable List block — the order-is-user-mutable half of drag-and-drop (the reorder intent). Each card pairs a key sequence with the order it leaves behind, renders the list there, and runs the same roving-tabindex / grab-then-move audit the CI conformance suite asserts. Plus a live interactive card: grab an item with the keyboard (Space, arrows, Space/Enter to drop, Escape to cancel) or drag it with the pointer — items relocate with the atomic Element.moveBefore(). A second section adds the Tier-2 cross-list scope (withCrossListReorder): cards move between sibling lists sharing a reorder-group key, Left/Right crossing lists. Backlog #130, #146.
description:
Concept

A live surface for the Reorderable List block, which realizes the reorder intent — the order-is-user-mutable half of "drag and drop" (moving a payload into a zone is the separate data-transfer concern in #007). For each shared fixture it shows the key sequence, renders the list at the order that sequence lands on, shows the produced HTML, and runs the contract audit — a green badge means the rendered list satisfies the verified accessibility contract.

Verified contract

The audit checks a native <ul reorderable role="listbox"> of role="option" items (each with a stable data-reorder-id and preserve-on-move), the DOM order matching the order model, and the roving tabindex invariant — exactly one item is tabindex="0" (the focused item) and every other is -1, with the grabbed item marked data-reorder-grabbed (never the deprecated aria-grabbed). The headline is the mandated keyboard path: WAI-ARIA APG grab-then-move with a polite live-region announcement of every move.

Cross-list scope

A second section covers the Tier-2 cross-list scope (withCrossListReorder): items move between sibling lists that share a reorder-group key. The order model becomes per-list, the roving tabindex spans the whole group, and Left/Right move an item across lists while the within-list reducer stays the inner loop for vertical movement.

No drift

The reducer, the renderer, the announcer, the audit, and the fixtures are the one shared source the CI conformance suite runs, so the badges here and CI exercise the exact same code. The renderer is a deterministic reference; production strategies (a real persistence backend, virtualization, drag styling) live in Frontier UI.

name: Validity Merge Playground
summary: The runnable home for the runtime customValidityMerge plug (#215) — the real window.customValidityMerge registry driving a form-associated <validity-merge-field>. Toggle the four named sources (native/schema/async/manual) live and watch them collapse into one MergedValidity pushed to native ElementInternals.setValidity, so the merged state drives :invalid / :user-invalid for free. Swap source-reduction ↔ last-write-wins with zero control edits. A runtime-conformance section asserts the plug's invariants (strictest-wins, pending-beats-valid, strategy swap, native validity binding, per-scope resolution).
description:
Concept

A live surface for the runtime customValidityMerge plug (#215) — the live counterpart to the dependency-free validity-merge/ model (#212). The real window.customValidityMerge registry, resolved per-scope through the injector chain, drives a form-associated <validity-merge-field> control. Toggle each of the four named sources and watch them collapse into one MergedValidity handed to native ElementInternals.setValidity.

Surface fixed, math swappable

Per #004 OP-1 the standard fixes the MergedValidity surface, not the merge math. Swap the strategy — source-reduction (native-first, strictest-wins) ↔ last-write-wins — and the same sources collapse differently with zero control edits.

:user-invalid for free

Because the merged state flows through setValidity(), the control participates in native :invalid / :user-invalid styling and form-submission blocking — the answer to the webvalidation open question: the UA owns the user-interaction gate, the control never re-exposes touched/dirty.

Verified contract

The runtime-conformance section asserts the live plug against its contract: strictest-wins, pending-beats-valid, the strategy swap, the native validity binding, and per-scope (nearest-wins) resolution. The strategies + orchestrator are the same source the #212 unit suite runs.

name: Validator Resolution Playground
summary: The runnable home for the runtime customValidatorResolution plug (#224) — the async sibling of validity-merge (#215). A real <async-validator-field> drives an async check that resolves on a controllable delay, so you can fire overlapping checks and watch the race: a slow, now-stale answer arrives last and the resolution strategy reconciles it — versioning drops the late answer, cancellation aborts the in-flight request — swapped with zero field edits. The surviving answer feeds a composed <validity-merge-field>'s async source so the merged :user-invalid styling reacts live. A runtime-conformance section asserts the plug's invariants (versioning-drops-stale, cancellation-aborts, pending{version}-then-terminal, cross-plane compose, per-scope resolution).
description:
Concept

A live surface for the runtime customValidatorResolution plug (#224) — the async sibling of the validity-merge plane (#215) and the live counterpart of the dependency-free validator-resolution/ model (#214). A real <async-validator-field> drives an async check whose answer resolves on a controllable delay.

The concept is a race

The plane only shows its value when answers arrive out of order. Raise the delay, then fire a stale race: a slow check starts, a fast fresh one follows, and the slow — now stale — answer arrives last. The race log shows the resolution strategy reconcile it: versioning lets the request finish then drops the late answer; cancellation aborts the in-flight request outright.

Policy swappable, surface fixed

Per #004 OP-1 the standard fixes the pending{version} → valid|invalid surface, not the stale-answer policy. Swap versioning ↔ cancellation with zero field edits.

Two planes composed

The surviving answer feeds the async source of a composed <validity-merge-field> (#215), so the merged state flows to native setValidity() and drives :user-invalid for free.

Verified contract

The runtime-conformance section asserts the live plug against its contract: versioning drops a superseded answer, cancellation aborts the prior request, the runner emits pending{version} then the terminal source, the async field feeds the merge field, and per-scope (nearest-wins) resolution. The strategies + runner are the same source the #214 unit suite runs.

name: Inline Emoji Picker (:-trigger)
summary: The first specific picker context per #370 Fork 4 / #591: a caret-anchored :-trigger popover for inserting emoji mid-text. Composes the inline-trigger detection block (#620, triggerSet=emoji) + the picker-surface results (#588) + expressive-symbol glyphs, with the text insertion adapter committing into a plain contenteditable. Detection-only, so it needs no full rich-text editor to run — it composes #590 for token insertion when present but degrades to text insertion. Backlog #592 (parent #586).
description:
Concept

The smallest concrete instance of the Inline Trigger engine: type : mid-sentence and a small caret-anchored popover lists emoji; arrow/type to filter, Enter to insert. It is the canonical worked example named by the Picker Surface (#588) and #591 as “a :-trigger popover anchored to the caret”.

The composition (no new mechanics)

  • Trigger — the inline-trigger intent with triggerSet: emoji (the : char), boundaryPolicy: any-prefix, queryPolicy: min-length (1) — detection suspends during IME composition.
  • Results — the picker surface listbox, rendering expressive-symbol glyphs (the picker shops them, never re-renders them); large sets virtualize via windowed-collection.
  • Anchor — the popover tethers to the caret (a virtual element) via the anchor intent; in contenteditable the DOM Range rect, in <input> a mirror-div measurement.
  • Insert — the text reference adapter replaces the :query range with the glyph (works in a plain contenteditable/<textarea>); the token adapter composes the rich-text standard (#590) where an atom node is wanted.

Accessibility

The APG editable-combobox pattern verbatim: DOM focus stays in the editable region, the listbox is driven by aria-activedescendant (no roving tabindex), and match counts announce through live-region-status. The native-first baseline degrades to a plain editable region with no inline suggestions.

Why detection-only matters here

Because the inline-trigger engine emits the resolved selection and owns no document model (#591 Fork 2-A), this context runs in a plain contenteditable today — it is composed with, not blocked on, the rich-text editing graduation (#618). The runnable page is a Frontier-UI reference impl; this entry specifies the context the impl realizes.

name: Expressive-Asset Shop (full-page catalog picker)
summary: The Fork-4 authoring consumer (#593): a dev/designer browses & shops the icon + emoji + sticker catalog inside Web Docs (#398) via a full-page filtered-collection instance of the picker surface (#588). The shop surface and the inline :-emoji picker (demo:inline-emoji-picker) are the two specific contexts of the one general picker — same surface, different density/trigger/container. Reads the open asset catalog (src/_data/expressiveAssets.json). Backlog #593 (parent #586).
description:
Concept

The authoring-time sibling of the inline :-emoji picker: where the inline context is a small caret-anchored popover for a runtime end-user, the shop is a full-page filtered collection a dev/designer browses inside Web Docs to find and copy an asset. Both are instances of the one Picker Surface (#588) — the same browse↔search composition at a different density, the see/shop paradigm of #236/#283.

The asset catalog (what this also defines)

The shop reads the expressive-asset catalog — src/_data/expressiveAssets.json — the open registry of asset sets (icon-set / emoji-set / sticker-pack) that exist for an author to shop: Material Symbols, Lucide, Heroicons (icons), Unicode emoji, and a reference sticker pack. It closes the gap that the icon intent carried a bare name: string with no catalog to resolve against. It is an open config registry (Config-Extends-Platform-Default): WE ships this reference vocabulary, a project extends it — no single set is mandated. Each set declares its own variant axes (Material Symbols' weight/fill/grade/optical-size, emoji's skin-tone/presentation) which map onto the icon and expressive-symbol intent dimensions.

The composition (no new mechanics)

  • Surface — the picker-surface 2-D browse grid ↔ 1-D search listbox hand-off, full-page; large sets virtualize via windowed-collection.
  • Filter — search narrows across the chosen set(s); category facets (kind, set) scope the grid.
  • Render — glyphs are rendered by the expressive-symbol intent (the shop shops them, never re-renders them); icons by the icon intent.
  • Shop action — selecting copies the resolved reference (set + name, or the sticker asset) for the author to paste — an authoring concern, not a runtime insertion.

Status

The catalog data + this context spec land here (#593); the rendered full-page Web-Docs surface and its runnable page are #398 / Frontier-UI impl concerns (status draft, no liveUrl yet).

name: Reveal Navigation — Conformance
description: Proves the reveal-nav composition recipe (#609 Fork 1A) — disclosure surface:anchored + anchor escape/menu + hover-intent — against the W3C APG Disclosure-Navigation invariants (disclosure-not-menu, no-reflow, i18n label-growth, native-first CSS baseline + JS enhancement).

name: Wizard / Flow Progress Playground
summary: A runtime proof that the wizard Block (<wizard-flow>, #691), the Flow Progress intent, and the Web Workflows protocol compose end-to-end in a real browser. Mounts the Block against a real CustomWorkflowEngine 4-step checkout graph: the engine owns orchestration (transitions, history, completion), the Block renders position + per-step status with aria-current and a 'Step N of M' announcement. Slice B of #651; the same Block + graph are exercised by the wizard unit suite and wizard-flow-demo.spec.ts so the demo and CI can't drift.
description:
Concept

The runtime demo for the reference wizard Block (<wizard-flow>) — slice B of #651. It proves the Web Workflows protocol, the Flow Progress intent, and the Block compose end-to-end in a browser.

What it shows

The Block is mounted against a real CustomWorkflowEngine graph (a 4-step checkout: cart → shipping → payment → review). The engine is the source of truth for position, history, and completion; the Block renders where you are and each step's status (wait/process/finish), driving aria-current="step" and a polite "Step N of M" live announcement via the Stepper behavior it composes. Next advances through the engine; Back uses the engine's history; Next disables when the final step completes.

No drift

The same Block + graph are proven headlessly by the wizard unit suite and an e2e spec (wizard-flow-demo.spec.ts) that drives Next/Back against this page.

name: Analytics Conformance Demo
summary: The runnable proof of the webanalytics CustomTracker contract+injector swap mechanic (#1012) — one call site issues the same identify()/track()/page()/group() calls while the resolved backend is swapped underneath via the CustomTrackerRegistry, so the application knows only the contract, never a vendor. Backends are in-demo recording stubs (a real GA4/Mixpanel/Segment needs network + credentials; the #1013 vendor adapters are the impl). A runtime-conformance section asserts the contract invariants live: no-op default, full routing, backend swap, Segment page arg order, per-call key routing, and unknown-backend throws.
description:
Concept

The runnable proof of the webanalytics CustomTracker contract+injector swap mechanic (#1012). One call site issues the same identify()/track()/page()/group() calls while the resolved backend is swapped underneath via the CustomTrackerRegistry — the application knows only the contract, never a vendor.

Recording stubs, not real vendors

Backends here are in-demo recording stubs — honest for a browser demo, since a real GA4/Mixpanel/Segment backend needs network + credentials (the #1013 vendor adapters are the impl). Only the contract crosses the seam; the demo never imports a vendor SDK.

Verified contract

The runtime-conformance section asserts the live registry against its contract: the native-first no-op default, identify/track/page/group routing, the backend swap mechanic, the Segment page(category, name) arg order, per-call trackerKey routing, and that an unknown backend throws UnknownTrackerError rather than silently substituting.

name: Web Reporting Conformance Demo
summary: The runnable proof of the webreporting report-model protocol (#431) end-to-end: three unrelated producer formats — SARIF, JUnit, and an Istanbul coverage summary — each ingest into one normalized Report pivot, then render through the same v1 renderers (findings table, coverage matrix). The core claim is the lossy-normalization-hub: the Report schema is the pivot the project never ships as a format, so a single set of renderers displays every tool's output (no bespoke viewer per producer). The export adapters (toSarif/toJUnit) close the loop — an export-then-ingest round-trips on the base vocabulary. A runtime-conformance section asserts each invariant against the live impl (#432 renderers, #433 ingest, #434 export).
description:
Concept

The runnable proof of the Web Reporting report-model protocol end-to-end: ingest a foreign producer's output (SARIF / JUnit / Istanbul coverage) → the normalized Report pivot → the shared v1 renderers (findings table, coverage matrix).

The normalization hub

The Report schema is the pivot the project never ships as a format — each foreign tool's output is ingested into it, so one set of renderers displays them all rather than a bespoke viewer per producer. The export adapters (toSarif / toJUnit) close the loop: an export-then-ingest round-trips on the base vocabulary.

Verified contract

The runtime-conformance section asserts the live impl: SARIF level→severity + location mapping, JUnit failure/pass severities, coverage file/metric scores pivoting into the matrix, the shared renderers producing accessible tables, and the export round-trip preserving findings.

name: DOM-less DI Host (display:contents)
summary: Confirms the layout-neutral provider-element pattern from the DOM-Less Composition research: a <di-scope> custom element that joins the injector tree to scope dependency injection but renders display:contents, so it adds no layout box — a flex/grid ancestor treats its children as its own. display:contents keeps the element in the DOM tree (the injector chain walks DOM ancestors, so a descendant still resolves the provider declared on the scope) while removing its box from layout: DOM-present, layout-absent. A conformance section asserts both halves live (computed display:contents, scoped provider resolves inside but not outside, children share the flex row equally) plus a control proving a plain <div> wrapper breaks the same row.
description:
Concept

Confirms the layout-neutral provider-element pattern from the DOM-Less Composition research: a <di-scope> element that joins the injector tree to scope dependency injection, but renders display: contents so it adds no layout box.

DOM-present, layout-absent

display: contents keeps the element in the DOM tree — the injector chain walks DOM ancestors, so a descendant still resolves the provider declared on the scope — while removing its box from layout, so a flex/grid ancestor lays out the scope's children as if they were its own.

Verified, with a control

The conformance section asserts the computed display:contents, that a descendant resolves the scoped provider while an element outside does not (isolation), and that the four flex items share the row equally across the scope boundary. A control proves the pattern is load-bearing: a plain <div> wrapper pulls its children out of the same flex row.
